cue: Each number is the sum of the two before it, starting from 0 and 1.
note: The recurrence is fib(n) = fib(n-1) + fib(n-2), with fib(0) = 0 and fib(1) = 1. Written as plain recursion it is O(2^n) because it recomputes the same subproblems; a bottom-up loop brings it down to O(n).

cue: Keep only the last two numbers and slide them forward one step at a time.
prompt: Write `fib(n)` for a non-negative integer `n` that returns the nth Fibonacci number, where `fib(0)` is 0 and `fib(1)` is 1. Build it bottom-up with a loop that keeps only the last two values, so it runs in O(n) time and O(1) extra space.
note: The recurrence fib(n) = fib(n-1) + fib(n-2) only ever needs the last two values, so carrying two variables gives O(n) time and O(1) space instead of the O(2^n) of naive recursion.

concept: ways to climb depend on the two smaller stairs
cue: The ways to reach step n are the ways to reach n-1 plus the ways to reach n-2.
note: To land on step n you came from step n-1 (a 1-step) or step n-2 (a 2-step), so ways(n) = ways(n-1) + ways(n-2). It is the Fibonacci recurrence with different starting values.

cue: Track the ways to reach the last two steps and add them to get the next.
prompt: Write `climbingStairs(n)` for a positive integer `n`. You climb a staircase of `n` steps, taking either 1 or 2 steps at a time. Return how many distinct ways you can reach the top. Build it bottom-up.
note: The recurrence is ways(n) = ways(n-1) + ways(n-2), with ways(1) = 1 and ways(2) = 2. Carrying the last two counts gives O(n) time and O(1) space.

concept: fill a cost table from the bottom
cue: Each entry adds this stair's price to the cheaper of the two entries below it.
prompt: What does the array `dp` hold at the end?
note: Here dp[i] is the least price to stand on stair i, following dp[i] = cost[i] + min(dp[i-1], dp[i-2]). The answer to the whole problem is the cheaper of the last two entries, since you can step to the top from either.

concept: cheapest way up a weighted staircase
cue: Roll two running costs forward, each time adding this stair's price to the cheaper one.
prompt: Write `minCostClimbingStairs(cost)` where `cost[i]` is the price of stepping off stair `i`. You may start at stair 0 or stair 1, and from a stair you climb 1 or 2 stairs. Return the least total price to step past the last stair and reach the top. For a length-2 array the answer is the smaller of the two prices.
note: With dp[i] = cost[i] + min(dp[i-1], dp[i-2]) as the least price to reach stair i, the top is one step past the end, so the answer is min(dp[n-1], dp[n-2]). Rolling two variables gives O(n) time and O(1) space.

concept: take this house or skip it
cue: At each index choose the better of skipping it, or taking it plus the best two back.
prompt: What does the array `dp` hold at the end?
note: dp[i] is the best sum using elements up to index i with no two adjacent, so dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2] + nums[i]): either skip element i and keep dp[i-1], or take it and add dp[i-2].

cue: Keep the best totals ending at the previous two positions and combine them each step.
prompt: Write `rob(nums)` that returns the largest sum of elements you can pick from `nums` with no two picked elements adjacent. An empty array returns 0.
note: The recurrence dp[i] = max(dp[i-1], dp[i-2] + nums[i]) needs only the last two totals, so two rolling variables give O(n) time and O(1) space.

concept: fewest coins for each amount up
cue: For each amount, add one coin to the best count for the amount left after taking a coin.
prompt: What does the array `dp` hold at the end?
note: dp[a] is the fewest coins that make amount a, following dp[a] = min over coins c of dp[a-c] + 1, with dp[0] = 0. Building from 0 upward means every smaller amount is ready when you need it.

concept: minimum coins to make an amount
cue: Fill a table from 0 up to amount; each entry is one more than the best reachable smaller amount.
prompt: Write `coinChange(coins, amount)` that returns the fewest coins from `coins`, each usable any number of times, that add up to `amount`, or `-1` if no combination does. `coinChange(coins, 0)` is 0.
note: The recurrence is dp[a] = min over coins c of dp[a-c] + 1, with dp[0] = 0 and unreachable amounts left at Infinity. Filling amounts 0..amount over all coins is O(amount * coins.length) time and O(amount) space.

concept: grid paths add the cell above and left
cue: The top row and left column are all 1; every other cell sums the one above and the one to its left.
prompt: What does the grid `dp` hold at the end?
note: dp[i][j] counts paths from the top-left to cell (i, j). You reach a cell only from above or from the left, so dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1]; the first row and column stay 1 because there is one straight path along each edge.

cue: Keep one row of counts and update it left to right, adding the value just left into each cell.
prompt: Write `uniquePaths(m, n)` that returns the number of distinct paths from the top-left corner to the bottom-right corner of an `m` by `n` grid, moving only right or down one cell at a time.
note: The recurrence dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j] + dp[i][j-1] only reads the row above, so a single rolling row works: before updating, row[j] still holds the value from the row above, and row[j-1] holds the value to the left. O(m*n) time, O(n) space.

concept: longest increasing run ending at each index
cue: Each entry starts at 1 and grows past any earlier smaller value with a longer run.
prompt: What does the array `dp` hold at the end?
note: dp[i] is the length of the longest strictly increasing subsequence that ends at index i. It starts at 1 and, for each earlier index j with a smaller value, can extend dp[j] by one. The answer to the whole problem is the largest entry.

cue: For each index, look back at every smaller earlier value and keep the longest run you can extend.
prompt: Write `lengthOfLIS(nums)` that returns the length of the longest strictly increasing subsequence of `nums`. The chosen elements keep their original order but need not be next to each other. An empty array returns 0. An O(n^2) solution is fine.
note: With dp[i] as the longest increasing subsequence ending at i, the recurrence is dp[i] = 1 + max(dp[j]) over earlier j with nums[j] < nums[i]. Comparing every pair makes it O(n^2) time and O(n) space.
